stem:
Two buildings stand on opposite sides of a straight, level street that is 60 feet wide. From the top of Building A, the angle of depression to the base of Building B is 53°. From the top of Building B, the angle of depression to the base of Building A is 37°. What is the sum of the heights, in feet, of the two buildings?

(tan 53° = 4/3 and tan 37° = 3/4)
explanation: For Building A, the angle of depression from its top to the base of Building B is 53°. By alternate interior angles, the angle of elevation from the base of Building B to the top of Building A is also 53°. In the right triangle with the height of Building A as the opposite side and the 60-foot street as the adjacent side: tan 53° = (height of A) / 60, so height of A = 60 × (4/3) = 80 feet. Similarly, the angle of elevation from the base of Building A to the top of Building B is 37°: tan 37° = (height of B) / 60, so height of B = 60 × (3/4) = 45 feet. The sum of the heights is 80 + 45 = 125 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Uses sin instead of tan for both buildings: height_A = 60 × sin 53° = 60 × (4/5) = 48, height_B = 60 × sin 37° = 60 × (3/5) = 36, sum = 48 + 36 = 84
distractorRationale.C: Assumes both buildings have the same height as Building A and doubles it: 80 × 2 = 160
distractorRationale.D: Computes only the height of Building A and stops, reporting 60 × (4/3) = 80

imageDescription: A cross-section diagram showing two buildings on opposite sides of a 60-foot-wide street. Building A is on the left, Building B on the right. A dashed line from the top of Building A to the base of Building B shows the 53° angle of depression. A dashed line from the top of Building B to the base of Building A shows the 37° angle of depression. The street width of 60 ft is labeled along the ground between the two buildings.
